mean, this is the best ways I've found to do it is we can just walk through.

So we don't have that many customers.

Most of
Likely, they heard about us through, oh, okay, first of all, it's almost always them reaching out to us, not us reaching out to them.
And that's partially because we do not have, it's immediately because we don't really have an outbound sales motion.

But the reason we don't have an outbound sales motion is we essentially only want to work, we want to self-select into only working with customers that are very hungry and committed to training frontier models.
And if they are hungry and committed to that, they're almost certainly going to reach out to us.
We don't want to convince someone to spend $10,000 or $100,000 or even a million dollars.
We want someone that is like, I have tens of millions of dollars, I'm ready to go, I need Surge.
So we basically wait for people to come to us.
And people come to us usually just through hearing about us through the word.
Until recently, know, I wasn't doing any podcasts or anything.
We recently got some reach out from like some two Chinese companies, Tencent and Alibaba that did come, I believe, from...
Edwin's recent publicity, but prior to that, it's almost all word of mouth in the industry.
So we'll get a reach out from one of two different types of people, either a researcher or engineer who actually needs data themselves to accomplish their own goals.
This is for sure preferred for us, or some type of AI data operations team, and they're responsible for identifying and sourcing new vendors for the company at large.
So we're either in touch with an engineering team or an ops team.
Again, we much prefer to be in touch with the engineering team so that can skip a lot.
It allows us to skip a lot of the bureaucracy.

For example, if we hop on a call with an engineer and they've already heard something positive about us, and Edwin's on the call, who obviously is very technically proficient in this space, and we can immediately get into, hey, what type of data?
What do you need?
What are you struggling with?
All the things you'd want to ask in a sales call, but we can do it completely genuinely because they're there because they actually have a need.
So it's like, what are you working on?
And they're like, well, I'm trying to solve X, Y, and Z thing, and I need a bunch of data that does A, B, and C.
And we start asking questions around, oh, did you want it more like this, more like that?
Have you thought through this?
And all of a sudden, we're just jamming for 25 minutes, right?
And at the end, they're like, these are the guys.
Like, I need these guys.
And so then they're driving, they become our champion, and they drive the rest of the thing.
they tell the contracting team, like, get something done with Surge.
Like, I want their data.
want to work with them.
It makes everything easier.
And what happens is, this is like a big piece for us.
By the end of the call, or shortly thereafter, maybe when we send them a sample of data, they implicitly understand that our data is high quality.
Because they either hear Edwin talk about it, and they think this is exactly the right way to think about it.
I already have trust in these guys.
Or they actually see data from us, either in the call, or we follow up a few days later.
They see data with their own eyes, they know viscerally this is high-quality data, and then they're good.
If we come in the other route, the AI data ops teams, for example, these people do not know what high-quality data looks like.
So all they can do is ask questions to us about how do you guys ensure quality?
And then whatever answer we give is essentially the same answer anyone else is going to give, which is, oh, we have a variety of rigorous, blah, blah, blah.
And I think a little bit to the branding point last week, we should and could have better talking points on what makes our quality stand out.

But it's almost like there's a very low ceiling on how good that conversation will ever get with the AI data ops folks, because they're only ever going to just be able to take in some static answer we give them and put that in a spreadsheet that someone else sees.
Like there's no life to actually seeing the data itself.
They're not as connected to the problem.
They're not as connected at all to the problem.
And they couldn't see, what they can understand is costs.
You know, a sales deck and case studies, but they don't understand the data itself.
So that's why we always prefer to go through, get introduced through engineers and researchers.
Does that all make sense so far?
Any questions there?
A ton of sense.
No, so far so good.
Okay.
So basically we have to get over a hump with either the researchers and engineers or the ops people of, hey, we're legit.
But, and what we try to do when we talk to the ops people is as soon as we can, we're like, show us an actual data requirement from the engineering team.
Put us in touch with the actual engineering team.
Sometimes they're down to do this.
Sometimes they're super like, no, you can't talk to them.
Like they're our customer, you know, they're, they're special, they're royalty.
You can't talk to them.
So we had to fight that battle.
being, like, where does that come from?
Is it like fear being replaced?
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
They're scared of being replaced.
They don't really understand what's going on.
And so their value add is like to keep the two parties that do understand what's going on separate from each other and try to.
But like pretend that they have value as a middleman.
But eventually we get to a place where we've showcased our abilities and everyone's like, yeah, the quality is good.
And pretty much like they're always like, yeah, this quality is really good.
Then the next step is usually, okay, well, how much does it cost?
And we share a quote with them and or our contract.
And we do a negotiation with the contract.
It's all the standard boring terms people negotiate, indemnity and liability and IP rights and all that stuff.
There's really nothing out of the ordinary interesting there.
The cost is like a hurdle we always jump through, which is we're higher quality.
We also usually cost more, not always.
And people are usually surprised at how much it costs at the beginning.
And they're like, whoa, like I was not expecting this.
I thought I was going to be like, I got a quote from another vendor.
That's like, you know, three times less or whatever.
And so all of a sudden now we're in this like weird apples to apples.
This thing where quality is this really fuzzy concept, cost is an incredibly objective concept.
And so it's really easy to compare on cost and really hard to compare on.
It's hard to say, does the cost differential match the quality differential?
You know, we have talking points around it, which I can get into, but they're just talking points and it's hard to prove out.
So at a certain point, I mean, we don't negotiate on pricing.
So at a certain point, it's just take it or leave it like, this is our pricing.
And that gives you leverage too.
It does.
And it usually works.
I don't think we've lost any customer that we wanted because they've never walked away just because there's like, the whole pricing model is too expensive.
So we have a come to Jesus moment on that and work through that.
And then usually define a set of projects that we're going to start with them with.
So it might be a particular type of capability they want their model to be better at, so they want a bunch of data there.
Or they might want our suggestions on what type of data to start with, in which case, you know, we'll analyze their model and give them some suggestions on where to start.
But we usually put together, like, a tranche of work to get started with.
So we're trying not to just sign a contract with no first project in sight.
We want a first project that's worth at least a couple million dollars, and say, okay, like, you guys expect to spend tens of millions, but this is a start.
This is a couple million, and now we can get going.
So we get all the contracts done, and we start working on the first project.
And then from there, it's a super organic process where we give them the first set of data, we give them some recommendations, and then things sort of just start flowing.
They train their models on the data, they see, oh, it's improving, and then some other teams, like, oh, can I get data?
And then the first team comes back and wants another round of their original request, and then things started to just start rolling.
We don't do a ton, something we want to get better at, but we don't do a ton of selling after that.
We sort of are more reactive to what they want to do, and then every now and then we'll nudge them in a direction or two.
But after that, it's sort of like on-demand, on-demand data, essentially.
Got it.
How are you coming up with the recommendations, like after the first round of data?
Yeah.
Yeah, this is a good question because I think this is something that we are opinionated on, that we could easily be in our sales stack, but it's not at the moment.
And it could sound fancier than it is, but we explain it very plainly.
But basically, what we propose to new customers is, hey, we want to run an evaluation on your model against whatever competitor you have your site set on.
So for most people, that's ChatGPT.
They want to be as good as ChatGPT.
And we'll run a wide...
Ranging evaluation.
So we'll create a bunch of prompts that like real users might put into a large language model across a whole variety of domains.
So marketing, computer science, and creative writing, and all these things.
Like any prompt that like you and your friends and your colleagues might put into the LLM on a given day that would help you out in your day.
We'll do some that are complicated and difficult, some that are simple and easy, some that include images, some that include requests for a web search, whatever.
And then we'll put those prompts into our customer's model and then ChatGPT.
And then we get responses for each of them.
And then we have our surgers evaluate those responses and decide which one is better.
So we do that over like maybe a thousand prompts or something.
And then we can pull out these insights where we're basically saying, here, let me see if I can show you this.
Yeah.
How are coming up with the prompts?
Um, we, uh, we have our surgers come up with them and we've
Basically do some diversity measurement to make sure that they're covering a wide range of topics and whatnot.
And the whole point, the whole point is that the prompt should almost be kind of, it should be real world.
And real world means messy.
Like you probably put a lot of, you know, fast typo-ridden prompts into an LLM.
It's not even perfectly clear what you want.
You're just like, ah, , let's do it.
Right?
Like we want that stuff too to see how it really works in the real world.
But let me show you what we end up producing.
So on that, let's see.
All right.
All right, so then we make these what we call insights decks, or like analysis report, and this is one we did for Llama a couple in March before they released a terrible model.
And so this is us comparing it to Claude 3.7, which was the latest Claude model at the time.
So we run this eval, and then we have this big old deck, it's like 70 slides, that we send over to them and sometimes walk them through live.
And we're saying, hey, look, Claude is beating you guys 50% of the time.
So 30% of the time, the two responses to the same prompt are rated about the same.
50% of the time, Claude is better.
22% of the time, Llama is better.
So if you think you're about to put out Llama and get a reaction like, oh my God,
It's as good as all the other models.
No, we're telling you that's not what's going to happen here.
Like, you guys are not doing as well as you think.
Or maybe you internally decide we're half as good as Claude.
That's fine.
Like, that's all we want to do.
Probably not.
But anyway, and then so we're giving them kind of the high-level statistics here.
And then we break it down also into specific domains.
Like, hey, let's look at coding in particular.
You're also losing pretty bad on coding.
It's even worse on reasoning.
Oh, my God, it's so bad on reasoning.
Like, that probably means you need more reasoning.
Training data, which we can provide them.
So this sort of this flywheel of, like, here's where you're weak.
And we can give you more data that helps.
And we get super specific examples, too.
Like, this is an example about creating a meal plan.
And we're, you know, annotating this response and calling out where the model's doing poorly and not.
And so we're giving them lots of detail-rich information, which builds lots of trust for us because it shows them that we potentially know their model performance better than they do.
Better than mom.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And so we try to do this early on in our relationships so that we establish that base of like, look, we know exactly what's going on with this model.
Now we can make database decisions on where you should invest your dollars for more training materials.
And when we can rerun this exact eval in two months after you've trained on a bunch of our data, and hopefully we should see all these numbers get better because you're hopefully getting better.
So that is how that informs our suggestions at the beginning of the relationship for what types of data they should gather.
And, of course, they have ideas, too, based on their own product roadmap of what's important to them.
It could be, you know, hey, we don't care about creating meal plans.
All we care about is coding.
Okay, well, then we can focus on coding.
Yep, yep.
But you start generally.
Oh, I got it's based on how you scope out the project at the beginning, right?
When you identify the first project to attack, it seems like it was a more general evaluation across all the different.
Yeah, that's our recommended starting point.
So I'll give you an example.
We're about to start working with Tencent.
And they have like five projects that they're interested in.
And then on top that.
Like, that's fine.
Totally fine.
We're not going to argue against that.
They want to do work.
But on top of that, we're going to do this general evaluation so that they can know if there's some other massive gap that they're not even aware of.
So those two things can run in parallel.
But this is, an important step most of the time.
And we also just do this periodically for current customers and just send to them and say, look, we did a bunch of insights on your latest models.
Like, take it or leave it.
But here it is.
How long does this take, the general insights process take?
Yep.
And what do you call it?
you have a name for it?
We don't have, like, a fancy name for it.
Well, like, the most basic thing we call it is, like, we're going to run an eval and we're going to create an insights deck.
That's what we would say.
Because we run these evals all the time.
So they're just called evals.
Most of the time it's customers asking us for them, not us offering them.
And most of the times it is not accompanied by one of these insight decks.
But we don't have a name for, like, hack when it does include insight deck.
Take care.
Anyway, so it's just running an eval.
Probably, like, when we start with Tencent, probably it will take us two to three days to check the prompts themselves.
Three-ish days, two to three days to run the eval, and then maybe three-ish days to create this deck.
So maybe like a week and a half from start to finish.
Interesting.
Okay.
Yeah, and then I'm trying to be good on time here.
So that makes sense to me.
How are you communicating that to the client?
Like, is that timeline you just gave me, the one you're giving them?
Like, what are they signing when they sign a contract?
Like, how are you scoping out timelines for the first project and the next five, if we're using the example you gave?
Like, what does that look like?
Everything stays pretty, for better and for worse, but usually for better.
We try to keep a lot of this out of contracts because it's quite fluid.
So what will happen is, you know, they'll send us a...
A spec and say, hey, can you run a project like this?
We'll give them a quote, say it's a million dollars and it's going to take us two weeks.
And the next day they'll say, great, start that, but also we want to make a change to this.
Instead of X, Y, Z, can we do A, B, C?
We're like, sure, that's actually going to make it like half as expensive and now we can get it done in a week.
Or the other direction, twice as expensive is going to take twice as long or whatever.
So we try to avoid situations where it's like, okay, now can we amend the contract to get approval for going twice as long?
So what it ends up being is basically big buckets of funds like, hey, open up a PO for $4 million for Tencent.
You want to do these projects.
We're going to work with you to get these projects done.
It might be more, it might be less than $4 million.
We're all just going to like stay in the loop on it and you can add more funds if you need to.
We're not like super hard committing to certain timelines and stuff.
It's just a little flip.
Yeah, no, this makes sense.
We give them a sense.
We give them a sense.
When we're doing ongoing collections.
So like.
We might run a project and give them data every week.
We usually just pick a day.
We're going to deliver to you every Friday.
We're going to deliver a batch of all the data we've gathered in the previous week.
So that's how it can work sometimes, too.
Got it.
And then, so this is all post-sale, right?
And we're helping you right now.
Awareness, marketing, messaging, essentially pre-sale, top of funnel stuff, top all the way down to bottom funnel.

But what part of the post-sale process that you just walked me through, do you think there is the most friction or room for improvement or challenges you see either now or what you foresee moving into the future?
There's two main challenges.
The first is ongoing price sensitivity.
So every time we give a quote, it's a chance for them to be surprised by the price.
So the pricing model is an hourly rate times the estimated amount of surge or time it's going to take to do a task.
So if
I think it's going to take an annotator one hour to do a task, and their rate is $75.
It's going to cost the client $75.
There's no other fee, no management fee, no consulting fee, nothing, no initiation fee.
It's literally just that.
And so every time they have a new project, we're going to have to say, we think this is going to take three hours, and so that's $225 a data point or whatever.
And they might say, oh, my God, that's so much more than I was thinking.
And we might have to have this back and forth on why it's reasonable that it would take three hours or ways we could simplify it to not make it take three hours.
So that friction pops up and is annoying.
One way for them to get rid of that friction is we always compare the dollars people invest in data to the dollars they invest in compute, and compute is probably like 100x data.
And so if we had some narrative that was showing the senior execs, the CFO of Google, whatever, that this is a tiny investment compared to your compute, you should not be.
Sweating the small stuff here, just like, this is fine to 5 or 10x, like, it's no problem and it's super impactful.
But instead, now, in the past year, people are a little bit more in a penny-pinching mode of like, ah, like, that's a little too expensive, whatever.
So that's one point of friction.
Other point of friction is, we have a platform where all this work happens.
Increasingly, customers are asking us to do work on their platforms.
Their platforms are usually terrible, with some exceptions, but they're mostly terrible.
And when we do work on their platforms, we lose the ability to do a lot of our quality work.
And that puts us in a tricky position, because we either have to say, no, we're not going to do this work with you, which does not go over well.
Two, we can do this, but because you're taking us off our platform, the quality is going to be worse.
That doesn't work well, because our entire selling point is quality, and we're charging quality prices.
And then the third option is to say, sure, we can do this, and the quality is going to be just as good, which then devalues our platform completely, right?
So we're always...

In this little weird scenario where we really want to do work on our platform because it helps us immensely operationally and it does allow us to use all our tools, what ends up happening, which we don't really communicate to customers, is, okay, when we're doing stuff on your platform, depending on how bad the platform is, yes, the quality is going to be worse, potentially moderately worse, at least a little bit worse.
We don't love saying that.

And the other thing is, this is going to create a ton of internal FTE ops overhead that we need to allocate to you now, and we don't charge for it because it's just not in our model at all, and it wouldn't even make sense to charge for it.
could never charge for it to the degree that it hurts us internally.
And so we just end up in this pretty weird...
Why does it hurt so much?
It hurts because they will, like, for example, be like, okay, you're going to...
So when we do work on someone else's platform, we don't see all the data before we deliver it back to them.
And so any data, any quality work that's being done is...

Essentially being done by virtue of the customer sending us a small sample of the data we produced, us like combing through it, and it's not on our system, it's just like a spreadsheet or something, combing through it, and then trying to figure out retroactively like who we need to remove from the project and who not to.
Like we can't be proactive really.
We have tons of monitoring on our platform, tons of routing of different surgers to different projects.
We can manage supply and demand.
Like it's all just, we can see all the data everyone's producing, which is super important, and see if they're doing good work or not.

And so because historically, and again, this goes back to the branding stuff, we've been secretive in a lot of ways in our company, but we've been secretive and ambiguous about what our platform actually allows us to do and what our secret sauce is.
People think, I can just spin up my own version, and I don't really know what I'm missing from Surge.
Like I know they hate it, they seem to really be resistant to it, but I don't really know concretely what's worse here.
So there's something missing there where it'd be amazing if there was this.
This industry understanding that, like, wow, Surge has, like, this amazing tool or technology that no one else has.
Don't even try to build it in-house.
Like, it's a fool's errand.
They've done something incredible.
I don't think people think that right now.
No.
I mean, how could they unless they're in the know, right?
Exactly.
That, I think, the perfect—I I could talk to, like, two hours about this.
I think that's the perfect cutoff for maybe another conversation strictly on the platform and the tech.
But this alone on the customer journey, super valuable because if you think about the messaging and who we're speaking to, it's your customers.
Like, it's high-intent people that are finding Surge, that are searching for Surge, and they're feeling all these pain points.
They want the value props that you just talked through and all of that.
So this was extremely helpful and clarifying in a lot of ways.
I also generally want to be cognizant of your time, right?
And so I don't want to keep taking a ton, but I think with every client we work with, right, these foundational resources, there's, like, a customer.
A discovery deep dive and a product deep dive or run through, they're what allows us to be so good foundationally.
And those two plus the pillars that we're making for you all is going to really let us hit the ground running and build off of for everything else.
So I'm hoping 30 more minutes is fine with you.
Yeah.
Yes.
Some other time because I have a call now, but yes.
No, yeah, yeah, yeah.
For sure.
We can grab time tomorrow.
have a big chunk of availability.
Sweet.
You want to just throw open the calendar now and find the time?
Yeah.
can send my link as well.
Are you free at 11?
PST?
Yeah.
Yep.
Yep.
Let's do it.
I'll send an invite.
Great.
Great, man.
I hope it was helpful for you to like get it out and communicate it as well because you have it down.
You have it down.
So now it's a matter of packaging that plus some of Edwin's thoughts.
Nick, I've
Hopefully, but you have it down very well, so it's helpful.
Awesome, man.
Cool, man.
Have a good rest your day.
We'll talk tomorrow.
Send me an invite.
Sending it now.
Yep.
Awesome.
Sounds good.
Yes, sir.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Deep dive into Surge AI's customer acquisition process and product offering details.](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Surge AI targets high-intent, well-funded customers (primarily frontier model labs) through inbound leads](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)
  - [Preference for direct engagement with engineering teams over AI data ops teams due to better understanding of data quality](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)
  - [Pricing is non-negotiable and typically higher than competitors, justified by superior quality](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)
  - [Comprehensive model evaluation process ("eval") used to guide data recommendations and showcase expertise](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1.0)

## Topics

### Customer Acquisition Process

  - [Inbound-focused strategy, no outbound sales motion](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=180.0)
  - [Two primary contact types: researchers/engineers (preferred) or AI data ops teams](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=180.0)
  - [Word-of-mouth driven, with recent increase from public appearances](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=230.0)
  - [Initial calls focus on understanding customer needs and showcasing Surge's expertise](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=286.0)
  - [Quality demonstration through conversation or data samples is crucial](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=336.0)

### Contracting and Pricing

  - [Negotiation primarily on standard terms (indemnity, liability, IP rights)](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
  - [Pricing often higher than competitors, causing initial surprise](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=529.0)
  - [Non-negotiable pricing model based on hourly rates](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=563.0)
  - [Aim for initial projects worth at least a couple million dollars](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=623.0)

### Model Evaluation and Insights

  - [Comprehensive "eval" process comparing customer's model to competitors (e.g., ChatGPT)](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=686.0)
  - [Uses diverse, real-world prompts across various domains](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=728.0)
  - [Produces detailed insights deck (70+ slides) highlighting strengths/weaknesses](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=853.0)
  - [Takes about 1.5 weeks to complete (prompt creation, evaluation, deck preparation)](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1099.0)
  - [Builds trust and informs data recommendations](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=960.0)

### Ongoing Customer Relationship

  - [Organic process after initial project, driven by customer needs](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=639.0)
  - [Periodic re-evaluations to track progress and identify new areas for improvement](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1042.0)
  - [Flexible approach to project scoping and funding (e.g., open POs for large amounts)](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1169.0)

### Challenges and Friction Points

  - [Ongoing price sensitivity with each new project quote](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1248.0)
  - [Requests to work on customer platforms, compromising quality control](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1432.0)
  - [Difficulty in communicating the value of Surge's proprietary platform](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1486.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Schedule follow-up meeting to discuss Surge AI's platform and technology in more detail](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1524.0)
  - [Explore ways to better communicate the value and uniqueness of Surge's platform to potential customers](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1498.0)
  - [Develop stronger narrative around data investment vs. compute costs to address price sensitivity](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1295.0)
  - [Consider strategies to maintain quality standards when working on customer platforms](https://fathom.video/share/6zyoEfZh2DwKADPu8AkmxQZ44wtr1Q8M?tab=summary&timestamp=1365.0)

action_items.description: Send calendar invite for 11am PST tomorrow to continue platform/tech discussion with Scott
